story_title: The Bre-X Busang Fraud — The Biggest Gold Scam in History
summary: Calgary junior miner Bre-X Minerals claimed to have found a 71-million-ounce gold deposit in Borneo, briefly becoming a C$6 billion company, before independent assays revealed every core sample had been salted with river gold and the deposit was worthless.
recommended_arc: Hook: largest claimed discovery in modern history. Rising: the world's biggest miners fight for a piece of Busang. Climax: chief geologist Michael de Guzman 'falls' from a helicopter; assays show zero gold. Payoff: billions wiped out, Canada's regulatory framework transformed.

verified_facts.claim: Bre-X Minerals was a Calgary-based junior gold mining company whose shares were listed on the Toronto Stock Exchange and the NASDAQ.

Bre-X's chief geologist Michael de Guzman and field manager John Felderhof announced in 1995 that the Busang site in Kalimantan, Indonesia contained a deposit estimated at 30 million ounces, later revised upward to 71 million ounces — which would have made it the world's largest known gold discovery.
At its peak in May 1996, Bre-X's market capitalisation reached approximately C$6 billion (roughly US$4.4 billion), making it one of the most valuable companies on the Toronto Stock Exchange.

In March 1997, independent assayers hired by Freeport-McMoRan Copper & Gold — which had been awarded a 15 percent stake in Busang by the Indonesian government — reported that their drill cores showed only trace amounts of gold, contradicting Bre-X's assay results.
Michael de Guzman fell or jumped from a helicopter on 19 March 1997 en route to Busang, four days before Freeport's damning assay results were made public; his death was officially ruled a suicide.

The Strathcona Mineral Services independent audit of May 1997 concluded that the Busang deposit was a fraud of 'previously unimaginable scale,' with core samples systematically salted with alluvial (river) gold shavings.

Bre-X filed for bankruptcy protection in Canada in 1997; investors including Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan, the Quebec public-sector pension fund CDPQ, and thousands of retail investors collectively lost an estimated C$3 billion.
